APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP task (sentiment analysis) and introduces a novel multimodal angle—augmenting textual features with cognitive (eye-tracking) signals—which is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences interested in multimodal and cognitive approaches. It uses standard problem formulation and evaluation (SVM/NB classifiers, F-score, accuracy, inter-annotator agreement) on established datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014), matching venue expectations. The main practical limitation—dependence on gaze/eye-tracking data—raises some applicability concerns but does not remove its relevance to the community, making this a good fit with minor scope caveats. | B(4): The paper addresses an NLP task (sentiment analysis) and proposes augmenting textual features with cognitive eye-tracking signals, which fits the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates using standard supervised classifiers (SVM, NB), appropriate datasets (Mishra et al. 2016; Joshi et al. 2014) and common metrics (F-score, accuracy), matching community expectations. The reliance on specialized gaze data and questions about practical applicability are legitimate scope concerns, so overall it is a good fit with minor reservations.

It uses standard problem formulation and evaluation (SVM/NB classifiers, F-score, accuracy, inter-annotator agreement) on established datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014), matching venue expectations. The main practical limitation—dependence on gaze/eye-tracking data—raises some applicability concerns but does not remove its relevance to the community, making this a good fit with minor scope caveats.

The reliance on specialized gaze data and questions about practical applicability are legitimate scope concerns, so overall it is a good fit with minor reservations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a genuinely new perspective by being the first to augment traditional textual sentiment features with cognitive features derived from readers' eye-movement data. It extracts a broad set of non-textual signals (fixations, saccades, regressions, gaze-saliency graphs) and demonstrates non-trivial gains (reported F-score improvements up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets) when combined with standard classifiers, clearly differentiating the contribution from prior text-only work. The requirement for gaze data limits immediate practicality but does not diminish the methodological novelty of integrating cognitive signals into sentiment analysis. | B(4): The paper introduces a distinct and concrete new perspective by augmenting traditional text features with cognitive features derived from eye-movement (fixations, saccades, regressions, gaze-saliency graphs) and explicitly claims to be the first to do so for sentiment analysis. Demonstrating consistent F-score/accuracy improvements across classifiers and two datasets (reported gains up to 3.7% and 9.3%) and analyzing effects on complex constructs like irony indicates a non-trivial extension beyond text-only methods. However, sentiment analysis itself is a well-studied problem and the reliance on eye-tracking data limits practicality, so the work is notably novel but not a radical paradigm shift.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a genuinely new perspective by being the first to augment traditional textual sentiment features with cognitive features derived from readers' eye-movement data. The requirement for gaze data limits immediate practicality but does not diminish the methodological novelty of integrating cognitive signals into sentiment analysis.

Demonstrating consistent F-score/accuracy improvements across classifiers and two datasets (reported gains up to 3.7% and 9.3%) and analyzing effects on complex constructs like irony indicates a non-trivial extension beyond text-only methods. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Based on the summary, the paper is well organized and clearly states its contributions early, with a readable writing style and explicit coverage of methodology (feature design, classifiers), datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics (F-score/accuracy), and stated limitations. Results (reported F-score gains) and related work coverage are presented, which supports clarity of the experimental narrative. However, the summary does not mention notation consistency, figures/tables effectiveness, or reporting of hyperparameters and code, indicating minor presentation gaps that prevent an exceptional (5) rating. | B(4): The summary indicates the paper is well organized (clear methodology, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics) and the main contributions are stated early (augmenting textual features with cognitive/eye-tracking features and reported F‑score gains). Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage as adequate, and limitations (practicality, hardware and aggregation issues) are clearly acknowledged. Minor presentation gaps include no mention of hyperparameter reporting or code availability and the summary does not comment on notation/figure quality, so I mark it as well‑written with some presentation issues.

reviewer_a.justification: Based on the summary, the paper is well organized and clearly states its contributions early, with a readable writing style and explicit coverage of methodology (feature design, classifiers), datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics (F-score/accuracy), and stated limitations. Results (reported F-score gains) and related work coverage are presented, which supports clarity of the experimental narrative. However, the summary does not mention notation consistency, figures/tables effectiveness, or reporting of hyperparameters and code, indicating minor presentation gaps that prevent an exceptional (5) rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary indicates the paper is well organized (clear methodology, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics) and the main contributions are stated early (augmenting textual features with cognitive/eye-tracking features and reported F‑score gains). Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage as adequate, and limitations (practicality, hardware and aggregation issues) are clearly acknowledged. Minor presentation gaps include no mention of hyperparameter reporting or code availability and the summary does not comment on notation/figure quality, so I mark it as well‑written with some presentation issues.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper is notable as the first to augment sentiment models with gaze-derived cognitive features and reports meaningful F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets) and improved handling of irony/sarcasm. This demonstrates a new multimodal research direction that could change how researchers think about incorporating reader signals into NLP. However, dependence on eye-tracking data and practical aggregation limitations (plus hardware/NLP tool errors noted) constrain immediate broad adoption, making the impact moderate rather than transformative. | B(3): The paper tackles an important problem by being the first to augment sentiment classifiers with cognitive (eye-tracking) features and demonstrates measurable F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on the two datasets). Its results and analyses—particularly improvements on irony/sarcasm detection—could shift how researchers think about leveraging reader cognition for challenging linguistic phenomena. However, the requirement for eye-tracking data and noted aggregation/hardware limitations constrain practical adoption and broader impact, making the contribution moderately influential rather than transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is notable as the first to augment sentiment models with gaze-derived cognitive features and reports meaningful F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets) and improved handling of irony/sarcasm. This demonstrates a new multimodal research direction that could change how researchers think about incorporating reader signals into NLP. However, dependence on eye-tracking data and practical aggregation limitations (plus hardware/NLP tool errors noted) constrain immediate broad adoption, making the impact moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important problem by being the first to augment sentiment classifiers with cognitive (eye-tracking) features and demonstrates measurable F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on the two datasets). Its results and analyses—particularly improvements on irony/sarcasm detection—could shift how researchers think about leveraging reader cognition for challenging linguistic phenomena. However, the requirement for eye-tracking data and noted aggregation/hardware limitations constrain practical adoption and broader impact, making the contribution moderately influential rather than transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates cognitive-augmented features across multiple classifiers (SVM, NB, rule-based) and two datasets, reports concrete F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3%), and includes feature-significance analysis and held-out experiments for irony/sarcasm, which gives reasonable empirical support for the main claims. However, ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported beyond feature significance, hyperparameters are not provided, and code is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility and deeper assessment of which cognitive features drive gains. Related work coverage is described as adequate and several limitations (gaze-data availability, hardware/tool errors, aggregation loss) are discussed, so the work is methodologically sound but missing some important experimental and reproducibility details. | B(4): The evaluation is reasonably comprehensive: the authors test multiple supervised classifiers (SVM, Naive Bayes, an in‑house rule-based system) on two established datasets and report concrete F‑score/accuracy gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3%), plus feature significance analysis and held‑out experiments showing benefits for detecting irony/sarcasm. However, ablation/sensitivity studies beyond the reported feature‑significance analysis are not clearly described, and important reproducibility details (code, hyperparameters) are missing. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the paper openly discusses practical limitations (need for gaze data, hardware errors, loss from averaging), so overall the empirical work is solid but has minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates cognitive-augmented features across multiple classifiers (SVM, NB, rule-based) and two datasets, reports concrete F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3%), and includes feature-significance analysis and held-out experiments for irony/sarcasm, which gives reasonable empirical support for the main claims. However, ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported beyond feature significance, hyperparameters are not provided, and code is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility and deeper assessment of which cognitive features drive gains. Related work coverage is described as adequate and several limitations (gaze-data availability, hardware/tool errors, aggregation loss) are discussed, so the work is methodologically sound but missing some important experimental and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: The evaluation is reasonably comprehensive: the authors test multiple supervised classifiers (SVM, Naive Bayes, an in‑house rule-based system) on two established datasets and report concrete F‑score/accuracy gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3%), plus feature significance analysis and held‑out experiments showing benefits for detecting irony/sarcasm. However, ablation/sensitivity studies beyond the reported feature‑significance analysis are not clearly described, and important reproducibility details (code, hyperparameters) are missing. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the paper openly discusses practical limitations (need for gaze data, hardware errors, loss from averaging), so overall the empirical work is solid but has minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Hyperparameters and training/configuration details are explicitly reported as not provided, which prevents exact replication. The preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, and although two datasets are named (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014), their exact versions/splits and availability are not clarified. Code is not mentioned and reimplementation would be difficult because many feature-extraction and aggregation specifics (eye-tracking feature computation, averaging/aggregation methods) are missing; while evaluation metrics are listed (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa), the evaluation procedures are not precisely specified. | B(2): Several critical reproducibility details are missing in the provided summary: hyperparameters and model training details are explicitly not reported, and code availability is not mentioned. The preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described (only a note about averaging eye-tracking features), and although two datasets are named (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014), it is not stated whether the required gaze data or full dataset specifications are publicly accessible. Evaluation metrics are listed (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa) but procedures (e.g., splits, statistical tests, training regime) are not precisely defined, making reproduction difficult.

reviewer_a.justification: Hyperparameters and training/configuration details are explicitly reported as not provided, which prevents exact replication. The preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, and although two datasets are named (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014), their exact versions/splits and availability are not clarified. Code is not mentioned and reimplementation would be difficult because many feature-extraction and aggregation specifics (eye-tracking feature computation, averaging/aggregation methods) are missing; while evaluation metrics are listed (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa), the evaluation procedures are not precisely specified.

reviewer_b.justification: Several critical reproducibility details are missing in the provided summary: hyperparameters and model training details are explicitly not reported, and code availability is not mentioned. The preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described (only a note about averaging eye-tracking features), and although two datasets are named (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014), it is not stated whether the required gaze data or full dataset specifications are publicly accessible. Evaluation metrics are listed (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa) but procedures (e.g., splits, statistical tests, training regime) are not precisely defined, making reproduction difficult.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its cognitive-feature-augmented classifiers against SVM, Naive Bayes, an in-house rule-based system and traditional-features-only systems on two datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014) using F-score and accuracy, which provides a fair and consistent comparison. It also includes feature-significance analysis and held-out experiments to analyze differences and reports statistically significant F-score gains, and the related work coverage is described as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate comparison to more recent/state-of-the-art neural baselines or explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably, so there are minor omissions in baseline coverage. | B(4): The paper evaluates its cognitive-feature-augmented classifiers against standard baselines (SVM, Naive Bayes, an in-house rule-based system and a traditional-features-only system) on two established datasets using F-score and accuracy, so comparisons are broadly appropriate and conducted under the same conditions. It further analyzes differences via feature-significance and held-out experiments (e.g., for irony/sarcasm). However, the summary does not report comparisons to more recent state-of-the-art (e.g., contemporary neural models) nor explicit acknowledgement of cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, indicating some relevant baselines are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its cognitive-feature-augmented classifiers against SVM, Naive Bayes, an in-house rule-based system and traditional-features-only systems on two datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014) using F-score and accuracy, which provides a fair and consistent comparison. It also includes feature-significance analysis and held-out experiments to analyze differences and reports statistically significant F-score gains, and the related work coverage is described as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate comparison to more recent/state-of-the-art neural baselines or explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably, so there are minor omissions in baseline coverage.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are experimentally supported: results across two datasets and multiple classifiers (SVM, NB, rule-based) report F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3%) and the paper measures inter-rater agreement, lending credibility to the empirical claims. However, important methodological details are missing or unclear in the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, code unavailable, and unspecified statistical-test details for the reported significance), and the acknowledged aggregation/averaging of eye-tracking features raises concerns about information loss and replicability. Overall the approach appears technically sound and the conclusions are largely justified, but minor methodological and reporting gaps prevent a top score. | B(4): The experimental claims are reasonably supported: the paper reports consistent F-score/accuracy improvements (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets) across classifiers and includes feature-significance analysis and held-out tests showing gains for irony/sarcasm. There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and the methodology uses appropriate baselines (SVM, Naive Bayes, rule-based) and relevant metrics (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa), but important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and aggregation of gaze features may obscure per-participant effects. Given these minor but notable issues, the conclusions are largely justified by the presented results but would benefit from fuller methodological disclosure.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are experimentally supported: results across two datasets and multiple classifiers (SVM, NB, rule-based) report F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3%) and the paper measures inter-rater agreement, lending credibility to the empirical claims. However, important methodological details are missing or unclear in the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, code unavailable, and unspecified statistical-test details for the reported significance), and the acknowledged aggregation/averaging of eye-tracking features raises concerns about information loss and replicability. Overall the approach appears technically sound and the conclusions are largely justified, but minor methodological and reporting gaps prevent a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental claims are reasonably supported: the paper reports consistent F-score/accuracy improvements (up to 3.7% and 9.3% on two datasets) across classifiers and includes feature-significance analysis and held-out tests showing gains for irony/sarcasm. There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and the methodology uses appropriate baselines (SVM, Naive Bayes, rule-based) and relevant metrics (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa), but important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and aggregation of gaze features may obscure per-participant effects. Given these minor but notable issues, the conclusions are largely justified by the presented results but would benefit from fuller methodological disclosure.

main_claims: Augmenting traditional textual features with cognitive features derived from readers' eye-movement patterns improves sentiment polarity detection.

Statistical classifiers augmented with cognitive features show significant accuracy/F-score improvements across classifiers and two datasets (reported F-score gains up to 3.7% and 9.3% on the two datasets).

Cognitive (eye-tracking) features help handle complex linguistic constructs such as irony and sarcasm, with feature significance analysis and held-out experiments indicating improved detection for such constructs.
This work is presented as the first approach to augment sentiment analysis with cognitive features derived from eye-movement data.
_summary.methodology: Augment traditional sentiment and sarcasm-related textual features with multiple cognitive features derived from readers' eye-movement (fixations, saccades, regressions, gaze-saliency graphs, etc.), then perform supervised classification (SVM, Naive Bayes) and an in-house rule-based classifier to evaluate the impact of the augmented feature set.
limitations: Method requires availability of gaze/eye-tracking data, making practicability/questionable in current settings.
Errors arise from eye-tracker hardware limitations and from linguistic tools/resources used (e.g., NLP tool errors).
Aggregating and averaging eye-tracking parameters (e.g., averaging graph-based features across participants) may cause loss of information and fail to leverage individual reader patterns.
